content.problemStatement:
Genjiko is one of the Japanese traditional games.
Chef Ciel will take place a variant version of genjiko in her restaurant.



The rules of the game are the following:



The challenger must be blindfolded.
Ciel feeds the challenger one of the **N** foods menus of her restaurant, **M** times.
The challenger must answer whether the **i**-th food and the **j**-th food are same or not, for all 1 ≤ **i** < **j** ≤ **M**.
For making the game difficult, Ciel cannot use the same menu for both the **i**-th menu and the (**i**+**j**)-th menu for all 1 ≤ **j** ≤ **K**. (The challenger knows this)

Ciel wonders how many patterns can be the challenger's answer.
Your task is calculating the number of the patterns modulo 1000000009 (109+9).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case contains three integers N, M, and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of patterns of the possible answers modulo 1000000009 (10^9+9) on a new line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first case is the same as genjiko. The second case is when Ciel cannot use the same menu consecutively. The third case is when Ciel can use 8 patterns. The fourth case is when Ciel can use no patterns.
Ciel cannot use the same menu consecutively.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming with memoization. The key insight is to calculate the number of patterns for each subproblem and store them in a memoization table to avoid redundant calculations. The algorithm works by iterating over all possible values of N, M, and K, and for each combination, it calculates the number of patterns using the memoized values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * M * K) per test case, O(T * N * M * K) total. Precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M * K) — the dominant structure is the memoization table.
